op.name: cumsum
op.summary: Return the cumulative sum of the elements along a given axis.

blocks.text: Return the cumulative sum of the elements along a given axis.

desc_blocks.text:
Axis along which the cumulative sum is computed. The default
(None) is to compute the cumsum over the flattened array.

Type of the returned array and of the accumulator in which the
elements are summed.  If `dtype` is not specified, it defaults
to the dtype of `a`, unless `a` has an integer dtype with a
precision less than that of the default platform integer.  In
that case, the default platform integer is used.

Alternative output array in which to place the result. It must
have the same shape and buffer length as the expected output
but the type will be cast if necessary. See :ref:`ufuncs-output-type`
for more details.

A new array holding the result is returned unless `out` is
specified, in which case a reference to `out` is returned. The
result has the same size as `a`, and the same shape as `a` if
`axis` is not None or `a` is a 1-d array.

blocks.text:
Arithmetic is modular when using integer types, and no error is
raised on overflow.

``cumsum(a)[-1]`` may not be equal to ``sum(a)`` for floating-point
values since ``sum`` may use a pairwise summation routine, reducing
the roundoff-error. See `sum` for more information.

blocks.code:
>>> import numpy as np
>>> a = np.array([[1,2,3], [4,5,6]])
>>> a
array([[1, 2, 3],
       [4, 5, 6]])
>>> np.cumsum(a)
array([ 1,  3,  6, 10, 15, 21])
>>> np.cumsum(a, dtype=float)     # specifies type of output value(s)
array([  1.,   3.,   6.,  10.,  15.,  21.])

>>> np.cumsum(a,axis=0)      # sum over rows for each of the 3 columns
array([[1, 2, 3],
       [5, 7, 9]])
>>> np.cumsum(a,axis=1)      # sum over columns for each of the 2 rows
array([[ 1,  3,  6],
       [ 4,  9, 15]])

blocks.text: cumulative_sum — Array API compatible alternative for ``cumsum``.
trapezoid — Integration of array values using composite trapezoidal rule.
diff — Calculate the n-th discrete difference along given axis.
